data.title: Falling Mortgage Rates Further Reduce Demand In The Housing Market (Not So Paradoxically)
data.description:
Waiting for even lower mortgage rates comes on top of all the big issues that crush demand in the housing market.

For the fourth week in a row, applications for mortgages to purchase a home fell, despite declining mortgage rates, and hit the lowest level since the week ended July 30, when mortgage rates were nearly 50 basis points higher. The much-ballyhooed idea that lower mortgage rates would unleash waves of demand is running into reality?

Demand by homebuyers began to plunge over three years ago and has been wobbling along very low levels ever since. Compared to the same week in 2019, purchase mortgage applications were down by 35%, according to data from the Mortgage Bankers Association today.

Applications for mortgages to purchase a home are an indicator of home sales in the near future.

But the average weekly mortgage rate for conforming 30-year fixed mortgages declined to 6.37% in the latest week, according to the MBA today.

Since the reporting week ended July 30, mortgage rates have dropped by 46 basis points (from 6.83%); and since the end of May, by 61 basis points (from 6.98%). Yet mortgage applications are now lower than they were then.

Mortgage rates, according to this measure, bottomed out in September 2024 at 6.13%, just before the Fed started cutting interest rates.

Not so paradoxical. The phenomenon that already low demand for mortgages would decline further even though mortgage rates have fallen substantially is not so paradoxical.

Potential homebuyers – those not turned off by the too-high prices – see mortgage rates zigzagging lower, and they listen to the housing prophets promoting the notion that mortgage rates will fall further, and they add 2 and 2 together, and the first thing they do is wait for even lower mortgage rates.

Waiting for even lower mortgage rates comes on top of all the big issues that have crushed demand in the housing market – including way too high prices, though they have started to come down in many markets, and in some markets by a lot, for single-family homes and for condos and co-ops.

Waiting for mortgage rates to decline further is the opposite behavior of the FOMO-driven buying that had set in late 2021 and early 2022, when mortgage rates had started to rise from below 3%, as the Fed was making noises about rate hikes and tapering QE to deal with inflation, which had begun to rage.

Fear of rising mortgage rates at the time, and efforts by homebuyers to “lock in” the still low mortgage rates and buy before prices spiked even further, triggered a huge wave of demand and crazy FOMO-driven buying, which resulted in home prices exploding through the first half of 2022.

So now mortgage rates have fallen, and prices are falling in many markets, and people are waiting for still lower mortgage rates and still lower prices.

But the lower rates increased refis. Applications for mortgages to refinance an existing mortgage ticked up in the latest week, and for the past six weeks have been higher than any time since March 2022. But that spike last month took a lot of pent-up refi demand off the table, it seems.

The September 2024 spike in demand for refi mortgages occurred when the MBA’s measure of the 30-year fixed mortgage rate was even lower than today.

Refi mortgage applications (red in the chart below) rise when mortgage interest rates (blue) fall, and vice versa.

That spike of refi mortgage applications in September was small compared to the boom years of the 3% mortgages that everyone was trying to refinance into. But the current level of demand for refi mortgages is in the middle of the range of three years before the pandemic.

data.title: S&P 500 Slips As Weak Earnings And Trade Jitters Test Market Momentum
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

​The S&P 500 fell 0.53% on Wednesday to close at 6,699, as disappointing corporate results and renewed geopolitical uncertainty weighed on sentiment. Futures were steady in early Thursday trading, with investors balancing mixed earnings against macro headwinds ahead of Friday’s key U.S. CPI report, which could set the tone for the Federal Reserve’s next policy steps.

Earnings results dominated the session, with Tesla sliding nearly 4% after delivering mixed numbers and cautious guidance, while IBM dropped more than 6% amid weak software revenue. The declines dragged the broader tech sector lower, offsetting gains in cyclical and travel-linked names such as Southwest Airlines and Las Vegas Sands, both of which posted upbeat quarterly results. Moderna, Lam Research, and Molina Healthcare also faced selling pressure, reflecting investor unease about the uneven pace of profit recovery across sectors.

The divergence between cyclical strength and weakness in technology underscores the market’s shifting tone this earnings season. While consumer and travel demand show signs of resilience, margin pressure and cautious forward guidance among major tech firms have kept investors selective, limiting the index’s ability to sustain gains near record levels.

Trade and policy risks weigh on outlook

Adding to the cautious mood were reports that the U.S. may expand export restrictions to China involving key software components, sparking a sell-off across technology shares. The move came just days before President Donald Trump and President Xi Jinping are scheduled to meet, an event markets hope will ease tensions. Trump later reassured investors that talks remain on track, which helped equities trim losses into the close, though traders remain wary of further policy surprises.

The geopolitical backdrop coincides with rising macro uncertainty. Friday’s U.S. inflation report is expected to show headline CPI rising 3.1% year-over-year. A stronger reading could reignite concerns that the Fed’s anticipated December rate cut may be delayed, while a softer print would likely boost risk sentiment.

Technical setup points to a holding phase

Technically, the S&P 500 remains inside a broad ascending channel that has guided its advance since April. Wednesday’s pullback brought prices back to test support at the 20-day EMA near 6,671, which has consistently provided a short-term floor. The 50-day EMA near 6,574 reinforces the next key support zone, with a break below exposing deeper downside toward the 100-day EMA at 6,404. On the upside, resistance remains firm around 6,842 and the upper channel boundary near 6,900–6,920.

S&P 500 price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Momentum indicators suggest the market is entering a consolidation phase. While the index continues to make higher lows, short-term sellers have regained control near the upper resistance levels. Volatility could increase heading into the CPI data, which is likely to determine whether the S&P 500 resumes its advance toward 7,000 or retreats toward its mid-channel support.

Outlook

As discussed in prior sessions, the index’s broader structure remains bullish so long as it holds above the 6,570 floor. The 20- and 50-day moving averages continue to act as critical supports defining the market’s intermediate trend.

For now, the S&P 500 is in a holding pattern, with the next decisive move likely driven by a combination of earnings momentum, inflation data, and central bank policy expectations. A dovish inflation outcome could lift equities toward fresh highs, while persistent macro and trade risks may keep rallies contained in the short term.

data.title: Macquarie Asset Strategy Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global markets delivered a strong 3Q25, driven by investors reassessing risks that had characterized the previous quarter. While reaccelerating inflation and weakening labor markets remained key concerns, robust public and private investment in artificial intelligence (AI) continued to buoy sentiment.

July provided a boost in optimism following the prior quarter’s volatility. Resilient US corporate earnings and signs of progress on tariffs helped stabilize markets. Notable policy developments included enactment of the One Big Beautiful Bill in the US and promotion of a pro-growth “involution” campaign (hypercompetitive price wars that have led to diminishing returns and widespread deflationary pressures) by China’s Politburo, both aimed at stimulating domestic industries and consumer activity.

Uncertainty about trade and immigration persisted, as many countries missed a key tariff deadline at the end of July. Labor market ambiguity also clouded economic data. Nevertheless, consumer sentiment rebounded in July from recent lows.

August opened with sharp equity losses as concern over a cooling labor market intensified, a trend that persisted throughout the quarter. Expectations for rate cuts increased following a weaker-than-expected July payroll report, and renewed AI investment plans helped markets recover from initial shocks.

US and European yield curves steepened in July and August amid heightened concerns about budget deficits, debt sustainability, and slowing disinflation. Meanwhile, US credit spreads narrowed. The quarter underscored a growing divergence in quality between corporate and government bonds.

Despite macroeconomic uncertainty, global equity and fixed income markets rose in September. US economic data indicated both a weakening labor market and a resilient consumer. After the Federal Reserve’s 0.25% rate cut in mid-September, expectations for further cuts fluctuated, but investors maintained a bullish outlook, further supported by ongoing AI investment.

Emerging markets outperformed year to date, including in 2Q25, benefiting from a weaker US dollar. Optimism generated by China’s Involution campaign provided additional stimulus.

Oil prices ended the quarter lower, occasionally rallying on sanctions and tariff threats, but ultimately weighed down by rising OPEC+ supply. Gold rallied as the market’s preferred safe-haven asset, offering greater insulation from inflation than US Treasurys.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Asset Strategy Fund generated positive returns but underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI ACWI Index (net).

The Fund’s underperformance was largely driven by the allocation to fixed income securities and stock selection within the equity portfolio. Fixed income, which provides critical correlation and volatility benefits in certain environments, is expected to drag in significant up markets, like the one experienced in 3Q. The Fund’s portfolio holds a meaningful allocation to gold, which was an area of strength and provided a tailwind to returns.

Stock selection proved challenging across most sectors this quarter with impacts from select stocks owned in the Fund and several highflyers that were not owned. This was particularly true within the consumer discretionary sector, where Chinese internet consumer platforms rallied, alongside Tesla (not owned) in the US, while performance among our luxury goods holdings was soft. Within technology, we had reduced our semiconductor exposure on strength, but the group saw continued outperformance amid AI tailwinds. In contrast, the debate continued for software, an area of exposure for the Fund that was challenged during the quarter. Within communication services, the Fund’s telecommunications stocks, normally defensive in nature, broke lower and trailed the benchmark while benchmark heavyweight Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(an underweight) surged.

Among individual stocks, Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argenaria SA (BBVA) (Spain), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Ltd. (TSMC)(TSM) (Taiwan), and KLA Corp.(USA) (KLAC) were the strongest relative performers BBVA, a leading banking franchise across Spain and Latin America, saw shares rally amid robust economic growth in Spain, supporting lending growth (alongside falling interest rates). The company also initiated medium-term targets through 2028, which were well received by the market.

TSMC, the world’s leading semiconductor manufacturer, rose amid robust demand for chips due to rising computing intensity of models with growing usage and reasoning capabilities. KLA is a supplier of semiconductor equipment, including inspection and metrology, critical for manufacturing semiconductors. The stock outperformed strongly in 3Q, driven by robust demand for semi equipment, in particular for advanced logic, high band memory (HBM) and advanced packaging, fueled by the AI infrastructure investments.

The greatest individual detractors were the Fund’s positions in Carrier Global Corp. (USA)(CARR) and SAP SA (Germany) (SAP), and an underweight to Tesla (USA) (TSLA).

Carrier Global is a worldwide leader in energy solutions, including heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC). Shares declined as the industry struggled to set proper guidance, resulting from a regulated refrigerant change in the residential HVAC market. We remain positive on the name given the company’s current transformation into a pure play HVAC and refrigeration company, which we believe should be an above-trend market over the long term. SAP, the German enterprise software maker, declined amid broader software sector weakness despite strong underlying business growth. The outlook remains positive, in our view, with a continued product upgrade cycle augmented by growing AI opportunities. Tesla, the multinational electric vehicle company, outperformed amid hype around its Robotaxi and Optimus potential. (Optimus is a general-purpose robotic humanoid under development by Tesla.)

Outlook

We maintain a cautiously optimistic stance on equities, with a focus on relative value opportunities. While a resilient earnings outlook in the US supports the broader market, US equity index valuations remain stretched compared with historical norms, creating a high hurdle for earnings growth and increased scrutiny of future catalysts. Despite recent gains, we remain constructive on non-US developed markets, where valuations remain more attractive and macroeconomic conditions are improving.

In fixed income, tight spreads leave limited room for credit outperformance. We continue to favor US sovereigns and investment grade bonds over lower-rated segments, which remain more vulnerable amid economic uncertainty. Overall, we see quality fixed income as a useful hedge in portfolios given lingering macro risks and potential for further policy shifts. Additionally, we continue to believe that real assets have the potential to serve as a hedge against rising equity volatility and lingering inflation uncertainty.

data.title: SailPoint: Sailing To Calmer Waters
data.description:
When SailPoint, Inc. (NASDAQ:SAIL) went public in February of this year, I concluded that I was comfortable to let this identity security play sail by. Its private equity owner Thomas Bravo decided to bring the pioneer of SaaS business models public, relatively soon after it was acquired, back in 2022.

That felt a bit soon to me, and while the company was not saddled with debt (at least not pro forma the IPO proceeds) the offering felt a bit rushed. In between demanding sales multiples and largely realistic break-even levels achieved, the company has seen growth continue at a healthy clip this year, with some realistic profits appearing here.

Amidst a stagnant share price, appeal has been on the increase, yet that does not mean that appeal is really seen here in my book.

An Identity Enterprise Security Play

SailPoint is looking to manage and secure dynamic access to both critical applications and data within organizations. The idea behind its solutions, is to provide a comprehensive identity security, by unifying identity data across systems and machines.

This is key, as IT staff are battling with an ever more complex environment, in which employees have to access multiple sources of data from various applications, both at home and at work, as well as through different devices. With access becoming much more complex, the risks of compromised systems is only on the increase, with managing such IT environments becomes every more important.

Founded in 2005, the company went public at in 2017 and was acquired by Thomas Bravo in a $6.9 billion deal in 2022, as the rise in AI and cyberattacks provided a real backdrop for the company to go public.

IPO Discussions

SailPoint went public at $23 per share in February, a level at which the 546 million shares were valued at $12.6 billion, with the enterprise valuation being roughly the same amidst a flattish net debt load.

These are demanding valuations if we look at the cold hard numbers. For the fiscal year ending in January 2022, revenues were reported at $450 million, with GAAP operating losses reported at $60 million. Revenues rose some 23% to $552 million in 2023, yet GAAP operating losses ballooned to $300 million, largely driven by amortization charges from the acquisition of the business. Revenues rose another 27% to $700 million for the year ending in 2024, with operating losses increasing further to $332 million.

For the year 2025, the company looked to be on track to post revenues around $876 million, marking solid growth, as operating losses for the first nine months of 2024 came in at $158 million, down over a hundred million on the year before, as finally some progress was made on that front. At the prevailing valuation, the company was valued at 14 times sales and if we add back amortization charges, the company was basically breaking even.

That valuation was too demanding and while AI might provide a driver in demand for its solutions, it could also invite fresh competition for the likes of SailPoint, all of which made me very cautious at the time of the offering. This was the case, even as some of its peers traded at demanding sales multiples as well, although often companied by real profits.

Trading Flat

After having fallen to the mid-teens in the spring, shares have seen a recovery to current levels of $22 per share, trading flat compared to the IPO day, lagging the market at large a bit.

In the spring, the company reported a 23% increase in full year sales to $862 million, as GAAP operating losses narrowed to $188 million, after fourth quarter losses were reported at $30 million. These numbers are impacted by a combined $222 million combined amortization charge, revealing modest profitability if we adjust for this.

The company guided for a 19-20% increase in full year sales to a midpoint of $1.03 billion, with adjusted income from operations seen moving up to $151-$156 million. This number compares to a $133 million number in 2024.

Following two solid quarterly reports in the first half of the year, the company hiked the full year guidance in two occasions, now seeing full year sales up to a midpoint of $1.055 billion, with adjusted income from operations seen up to a midpoint of $179 million.

Note that first quarter earnings were impacted by elevated stock-based compensation expenses in connection to the IPO. A GAAP net loss of $10 million was reported in the second quarter, yet if we add back pre-tax amortization charges of a some $46 million, realistic earnings of around $36 million (albeit for taxes) emerged.

With 555 million shares trading at $22 per share, the market value of the firm stands at $12.2 billion. This includes a modest net cash position of $271 million now, for a $12 billion enterprise valuation. In comparison to the full year sales outlook, sales multiples have come down to 11 times, while the company has begun to report some real profits, although these are just cents on the dollar here.

What Now?

Compared to the situation in February, I am turning a bit more upbeat. In between a flattish valuation, the company has seen growth rates around 30% so far this year, while having demonstrated on operating leverage. At this point, realistic profits are now achieved, although minimal in relation to the prevailing valuation.

Resulting sales multiples have come down a lot, as growth, at least for now, is aided by the rise of AI agents and machine entities, but over time, AI inspired cybersecurity solutions could provide a more competitive threat as well.

As a result, I see appeal increasing a bit, but most of this is relative appeal. While real profitability is achieved here, these earnings are so minimal that no expected backing is seen from a valuation point of view anytime soon, at least not based on realistic earnings metrics.

data.title: First National Bank Alaska: Time To Engage The Final Frontier
data.description:
The financial sector has many different classifications when it comes specifically to banking institutions. There is a massive size variety in banking institutions. The Federal Reserve must stress test banks to ensure they are sufficiently risk-tolerant and able to survive even the worst of economic situations. During the Great Financial Crisis, a number of financial institutions that appeared strong on the outside collapsed because of their extreme risk-taking in the name of capital gains.

U.S. banking regulations contain four different categories of financial institutions. These would be from the largest of financial institutions that are considered to be globally systemically important banks, all the way down to financial institutions that have over $100 billion worth of total assets. The Federal Reserve has its own labeling system:

  • Global Systemically Important Banks
  • Large Banking Corporations
  • Regional Banking Organizations
  • Community Banking Organization

Recently, I covered an attractive preferred security that would fall into the category of a regional banking organization, because the financial institution Bank OZK (OZK) has over $10 billion of assets but less than $100 billion.

Today, I want to take some time and explore an opportunity that falls strictly into the community banking organization category. This would be a financial institution that is extremely focused on a locality, having less than $10 billion in assets, often meaning that they only have a handful of branches. These types of opportunities can also provide excellent opportunities for income or returns. These smaller banking organizations are usually laser-focused on shareholder returns and on providing exceptional quality service to their customers. If you have to compete against major banks that have billions of dollars worth of assets at their disposal at any given moment, as a small financial institution, you must provide an exceptional experience and have wonderfully strong underwriting to be able to compete effectively.

Let's dive in!

The Final Frontier

Alaska is commonly nicknamed the Last Frontier, largely because it is still heavily unpopulated and exceptionally wild. Alaska may seem like an unusual place to find a wonderful income opportunity, but institutions there are as rugged and as virtuous as the people who live there. You may think of Alaska mainly as grizzly bears and oil. But it's important to remember that among all the activity that goes on there, a financial institution of high quality must be present to provide the liquidity that's needed.

This is when we will turn our attention to Alaska's largest community bank - First National Bank Alaska (OTCQX:FBAK). FBAK was founded in 1922, and recently celebrated 100 years of continual operation. In this time, it has grown from one single location to 28 locations within Alaska.

When looking at a community-sized bank, I want to see that the financial institution not only has strong growth over its lifespan but is also recognized for strong customer service. Like I mentioned, if you are only operating on less than $10 billion of assets, you must have a stellar customer-focused experience; otherwise, you'll die out relatively quickly or be bought out by a larger firm. The fact that FBAK has been able to exist for 100 years and has not been taken over by a larger institution wanting to make a move into Alaska tells me that there is a reason for this.

FBAK has succeeded in winning multiple recognitions:

In 2025, Alaska Business readers voted First National “Best of Alaska Business” in the Best Place to Work category for the 10th year in a row, Best Bank/Credit Union for the fifth time, and Best Customer Service for the second year in a row. That year, Forbes also selected First National as the sixth best bank on their America’s Best Banks list and one of the top two Banks in the State, and Newsweek recognized the bank as one of the nation’s Best Regional Banks and Credit Unions. The bank was also voted “Best of Alaska” in 2024 in the Anchorage Daily News awards, ranking as one of the top three in the Bank/Financial category for the sixth year in a row. American Banker again recognized First National as a “Best Bank to Work For” in 2024, for the seventh consecutive year.

Recognizing that FBAK is well-liked by not only their employees but also their community, and their performance as a financial institution ranks it among the best, according to Forbes. It is easy to see why they have remained a standalone, independent financial institution. When Wells Fargo (WFC) decided to make an entrance into the Alaska market in a big way, it did so by buying out one of their peers.

Financial Performance

Now that we've taken a moment to explore some of the intangibles about why FBAK continues to exist and confirmed that it is a well-loved community bank, we must now take a look at its tangible financial performance.

Looking over the last four quarters of performance, we can see that the deposits within FBAK have dipped slightly. Meanwhile, their loan book has also grown slightly simultaneously. FBAK is still well capitalized; their loan-to-deposit ratio is still around the 71% mark. Historically, a ratio below 80% is considered a highly conservative approach.

This conservative nature is mirrored in their non-performing loan history. Historically, it has been in the 0.10-0.20% range. FBAK saw a spike this quarter to 0.38%. They maintain an allowance of 0.77%, meaning this spike is not a problem now, but should be watched going forward. To put this into perspective, Regions Financial (RF) sees a range of 0.70-0.80% in its non-performing loans reported. JP Morgan Chase (JPM) reported a range of 0.60-0.70%.

This once again highlights and underscores that FBAK is extremely conservative, not only in their originations and underwriting, but also in the level of capital that they leave exposed to lending. Lending will be the primary driver of returns for a financial institution of this size, and FBAK can carefully mitigate potential damage by being extremely selective in the loans that it originates.

This careful approach has allowed FBAK to steadily grow its earnings per share without exposing shareholders to extensive risk.

The Dividend

When I'm looking for an investment that will reward me for my ownership, I want it to primarily do so through the income it provides me via dividends and distributions. Financial institutions are historically recognized for having growing dividends but also low yields.

Often, institutions that have higher yields are riskier, largely because of the risk they've taken on or because of negative market sentiment that has driven down the share price, raising the yield. However, when you move out of the major financial institution, starting to climb your way down the ladder below the $100 billion mark, you can find higher yields simply because they are unrecognized or less well-known. With assets only in the $4 billion range, FBAK falls squarely into the relatively unknown category, and its yield of 7% is squarely covered by earnings.

Last quarter, FBAK reported an EPS of $5.80 while declaring a $4 per share dividend, 68% of earnings paid to their shareholders.

Over the years, FBAK has steadily grown its dividend, along with paying frequent year-end special dividends:

FBAK, like many financial institutions, is clearly a reliable, qualified dividend income-paying company.

Risks To Consider

Because FBAK is a community bank with exposure directly to the Alaskan state economy, the biggest risk that comes from this is some sort of economic calamity occurring specifically in Alaska. Many financial institutions of larger sizes can balance out the economic struggles of one area with the successes of another due to their exposure to both. But FBAK is solely focused on Alaska itself, and the health of the Alaskan economy is of essential importance.

At this point, the Alaskan economy continues to be strong. Overall, GDP growth in Alaska is slower than the 10-year average, but personal income growth is higher. This means that while the economy itself is not producing more growth than it has historically, individuals have more free income that they're generating to enjoy.

The other major issue to consider when trying to buy shares of FBAK is its extremely low volume. This can be readily mitigated by a healthy dose of patience and a judicious usage of a limit order.

Looking Forward

FBAK is expected to announce their earnings at the end of October or the very beginning of November, historically between Oct. 29 and Nov. 5. When reviewing the next report, I will be looking at their loan-to-deposit ratio and non-performing loans. Interest rate cuts historically spur more lending activity and reduce deposits - lower rates encourage spending. FBAK has historically kept a very conservative ratio and has room to grow their loan book effectively, even if deposits fall slightly.

A major concern would be if non-performing loans climbed higher. They have a strong cushion for their nonperforming allowance, but my preference is to see this percentage fall, not climb again.

Conclusion

FBAK is a well-run, highly conservative community bank offering 7% yields to new shareholders at current prices. It would find a ready home in one's taxable brokerage account due to the QDI that it pays out quarterly.

Investors looking to initiate a position in FBAK are strongly encouraged to use a limit order along with a massive dose of patience. It can take time to fill your order successfully, and if you want a very specific price or price range, being careful in setting that limit order can reward you over the long run.

When it comes to retirement, investing in the financial success of various sectors of the United States economy can provide you with wonderful returns over the long run. But careful examination of each opportunity is essential to ensure that it aligns with your desired goals and outcomes. There are millions of dollars traveling through the market daily and coming to the doors of investors just like you in the form of dividends and distribution checks. If you were not receiving those regularly, but you're struggling to make ends meet, today might be the time to adjust your portfolio.

data.title: Harding Loevner Global Equity Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
What's on Our Minds

The past six months were one of the strongest periods for momentum investing in more than 70 years. Since the start of the year, high-momentum stocks have outperformed low-momentum stocks by an extraordinary 45 percentage points.

The surge has been fueled in large part by artificial intelligence (AI), a powerful technological innovation responsible for significant divergences in equity performance, especially in the US. Enthusiasm for a narrow set of perceived AI winners has propelled indexes higher, while other stocks have generally languished. The S&P 500’s returns are now more concentrated than they were during the dotcom bubble. US retail investors are also active again and piling into expensive stocks (echoing a trend observed during the “memestock” mania of 2021). Concentrated markets heighten investor attention on a limited set of companies, which can fuel momentum and amplify the fear of missing out.

These are challenging dynamics for our portfolio, which is designed to emphasize diversification and underpriced securities of high- quality, growing businesses—not thematic investment. Diverse sources of growth reduce the risk of any individual bet, and history shows that market leadership rarely persists across regimes. The Nifty Fifty stocks of the 1970s ceded their market-leading returns to the global oil majors in the 1980s, which soon lost ground to Japanese Financials, and so on. Even technology as revolutionary as the internet didn’t deliver lasting gains for shareholders of Cisco, Intel, and Lucent, some of the world’s most valuable companies during the technology-led rally of the late 1990s.

Does the portfolio’s performance shortfall indicate that this time is ditterent, that it’s time to concentrate the portfolio in hot stocks?

Not only would that run counter to our insistence on diversification, which is grounded in market history, but also, as we discussed in our letter for the third quarter of 2024, we have deliberately resisted incorporating simple price momentum as a factor in our investment process. One reason is that serial correlation of share-price changes bears only a weak connection to company fundamentals. Chasing stocks that have already outperformed, or selling those that have already underperformed, also carries real-world costs—both the actual trading costs and in increased turnover of a portfolio—and runs the risk of sharp reversals.

In the past, buying weak stocks of strong companies was an approach that worked well for the portfolio. Much of its alpha from 2010 to 2020 came from shares of companies with modestly negative momentum. However, in the past three years, the tendency for share prices to continue in their recent trading patterns has become unusually strong.

AI, in our view, is a real secular change that is going to reshape industries. But it also represents a broad source of uncertainty in today’s markets. Its impact could be incremental or profoundly disruptive, and that breadth of possibilities leaves historical company fundamentals as a less reliable guide to long-term future value. Markets have instead leaned on narratives, rewarding companies perceived as central to the buildout of AI and penalizing those that don’t fit the story. Oracle, a stock we don’t hold, and The Trade Desk (TTD), one that we do, highlight the divergence.

Oracle (ORCL) is a mature enterprise-software and cloud-services provider whose shares were lagging this year, until a detail in its most recent earnings report instantly shifted the market’s perception of the business. Remaining performance obligations, or the company’s order backlog, grew nearly 360% from a year ago to US$455 billion, as customers look to secure access to cloud-computing services to support their AI needs. Oracle’s stock surged 36% on September 10, its largest single-day gain since 1992 (when its market capitalization was a fraction of the size that it is today). News reports later revealed that a US$300 billion commitment from OpenAI (OPENAI) represents the majority of the order backlog. OpenAI’s products may be at the center of the AI transition, but it’s still a private startup that is projected to generate only US$13 billion in revenues this year and has ongoing losses. Therefore, fulfilling its commitment to Oracle (and others with which it has struck similar agreements) likely hinges on its ability to raise a substantial portion of the money, a risk that oddly didn’t seem to faze markets.

AI, in our view, is a real secular change that is going to reshape industries. But it also represents a broad source of uncertainty in today’s markets.

Now take The Trade Desk. Companies use its digital platform to purchase advertising space on websites, streaming-TV apps, digital billboards, and more. The company projected 14% revenue growth for the third quarter, modestly below the market’s expectations. Even though 14% is still a robust growth rate by most standards, the muted forecast caused the stock to fall sharply. Some of the concerns are valid. The Trade Desk faces rising competition from Amazon.com, and there’s the possibility that banner ads on the open internet are eventually overtaken by AI chatbots, which could increasingly become the gatekeepers to websites and lace ads into their own text responses. However, we think the company is well positioned to benefit from the secular growth in digital advertising. Its platform is differentiated, not just in the underlying technology, but also because it is independent, unlike large media companies such as Alphabet or Meta Platforms (META), which funnel ad dollars to their own apps and services. The Trade Desk’s reach is also strong with streaming-TV services, and so as more viewers cut the cord, the roughly US$150 billion that advertisers spend on traditional TV will likely continue making its way into The Trade Desk’s coffers.

Market Snapshot

Global equity markets rose 7.7%, led by AI-related stocks in the Information Technology (IT) and Communications Services sectors.

China was the best-performing major market, overcoming fears about high tariff rates and trade uncertainty. Renewed enthusiasm for stocks such as Alibaba and Tencent, which received government encouragement to keep investing in local AI capabilities, also buoyed the Chinese market.

The Materials sector was helped by M&A activity and mine closures that increased commodity stock valuations.

Consumer Staples and Health Care, sectors that are overrepresented in quality indexes, underperformed as part of a broader trend in which the stocks of high-quality companies underperformed those of low-quality companies.

Developed Markets were hurt by weak returns in Europe, primarily Germany, which gave back some of its gains from earlier in the year, when the German government announced its historic fiscal stimulus plan.

The contrast is instructive. Oracle was rewarded for suddenly having an AI story to tell. The Trade Desk may have been punished to an excessive degree because it doesn’t have one, even though its core business is growing healthily and there’s a reasonably compelling case for that to continue. In both instances, speculation and momentum seemed to contribute to outsize price moves.

We are alert to signs that some of the market’s AI-related momentum could lose steam. The industry is engaging in circular funding, in which large data-center operators and chipmakers have taken equity stakes in their customers, the developers of AI models, so that those customers can attord to keep purchasing computing power. This seems to be an unsustainable source of cash flow for the model developers. NVIDIA’s (NVDA) plan to invest in OpenAI is a recent example of what has become an industry-wide phenomenon: Microsoft (MSFT) has a stake in OpenAI, while Alphabet (GOOG) and Amazon (AMZN) have each taken stakes in Anthropic. Meanwhile, OpenAI, in addition to its unfunded commitment to Oracle, has made sizeable commitments to AMD (AMD), CoreWeave (CRWV), NVIDIA, and Microsoft, raising questions about its ability to remain a prime source of revenue for the biggest enablers of its technology.

There is a massive amount of capital pouring into AI infrastructure, not just from the large, highly profitable data-center operators— whose capex is expected to climb 45% from last year’s already elevated levels—but also from more speculative sources of funding, including private-credit and venture-capital markets. Similar to what happens after large acquisitions, aggressive increases in organic investment tend to be followed by stock-price underperformance in the years that follow. That’s because the returns on investment for extravagant products often disappoint, a tendency perhaps partly attributable to overconfident management teams. The risk is amplified when valuations are expensive, capex is externally financed, and there is a lot of controversy around a stock, as measured by volatility. Research also shows that growth stocks have an asymmetric return profile: since they tend to be pricier, they have further to fall when there’s a negative earnings surprise. When this happens, it’s called an “earnings torpedo,” a term coined by Douglas Skinner and Richard Sloan in 1999. The aggressive capital spending on AI and heavy outside funding, if combined with high enough stock prices, could set the stage for a market-wide torpedo.

There is a massive amount of capital pouring into AI infrastructure, not just from the large, highly profitable data-center operators but also from more speculative sources of funding, including private-credit and venture-capital markets.

One important difference between the dotcom bubble and what we’re seeing today is that the main spenders this time have stupendous financial strength. Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft together are expected to spend more than US$350 billion this year while boasting an aggregate free-cash-flow margin of 13%, roughly two times greater than the average margin for non-tech companies in the US.

For now, so long as market returns remain overwhelmingly concentrated in a few momentum stocks, the portfolio will likely lag. However, history shows that diversification and valuation discipline are vital to long-term outperformance. Market leadership goes through cycles, stories evolve, and extravagant capital-intensive pursuits, on average, destroy value. A portfolio characterized by fundamental business strength and prudent diversification offers resiliency against such risks and therefore should deliver superior returns over the long run.

Portfolio in Focus

Although the market appears to be almost singularly focused on “AI winners,” the companies anointed with the label continues to change. Bank of America’s US AI Beneficiaries Index already has had 70% turnover this year. For example, a few months ago, markets feared that Alphabet’s search business would be made obsolete by AI chatbots such as ChatGPT, and the company did appear to be a step behind when it clumsily rolled out Bard, a consumer-facing chatbot, in 2023. However, Alphabet’s AI offering for businesses is rapidly gaining traction. Its Gemini models now have an enterprise user base that’s triple the size of ChatGPT’s. Thanks to that progress and a strong technical offering, Alphabet has moved back into the winners’ ranks, for now.

Even as AI reshapes industries, it may not disrupt the strong, very entrenched market leaders to the degree some people fear. Some companies appear to be positioned well enough that the idea of them being distracted and unable to profit from the AI trend themselves is an overly pessimistic view. Alphabet may be one example.

Markets have now pinpointed various software and IT-services companies as likely victims of AI. The portfolio holds Accenture (ACN), the leading global provider of IT services, with almost 800,000 employees and annual revenues of US$70 billion. There is debate over whether technological change will commoditize its business, reduce barriers to entry, and lower the margins of incumbents, or instead allow Accenture to capture new revenue streams. Management seems to recognize the significance of generative AI for its industry (dinosaurs saw the meteor, bears will say). Therefore, it’s racing to modernize the business with the goal of delivering, and eventually profiting from, AI functionality across product lines. The question is whether Accenture can evolve fast enough; its progress so far is encouraging.

Even as AI reshapes industries, it may not disrupt the strong, very entrenched market leaders to the degree some people fear.

Accenture’s AI bookings doubled to US$5.9 billion in its fiscal year that ended in August. Accenture also remains the number one consultant for Google, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Oracle, and NVIDIA, and business with its key partners grew 9% in fiscal 2025. The company’s competitive advantage stems from these key relationships and the breadth of its domain expertise rather than just having armies of computer programmers. It has a uniquely skilled workforce, with 77,000 AI and data specialists, and the capacity to invest further in its people and technological capabilities to stay competitive.

Despite the market’s fixation on AI, the AI industry doesn’t have a monopoly on earnings growth. The portfolio holds many companies supported by an array of non-AI-dependent secular growth themes. The Health Care sector, for example, despite meaningfully detracting from relative returns this year, features a number of businesses that otter durable growth and high returns on investment.

The portfolio’s Health Care holdings benefit from several long-term trends that support a multi-year earnings growth runway. In the US, growth in national health expenditures is projected to outpace that of GDP over the next decade, lifting the sector’s share of the economy above 20% by 2033. Globally, populations are aging rapidly; by 2030, one in six people will be 60 or older, with those over 80 set to triple by 2050. Innovation also remains robust and broadly diversified. After a post-pandemic lull, the Food and Drug Administration approved 50 novel drugs in 2024, and biosimilars are being approved at a record pace, all of which improves visibility for new products in oncology, metabolic disease, neurology, and rare disorders. Technologies geared to improve health procedures—from robotics to minimally invasive devices—along with data-driven diagnostics continue to expand addressable markets and companies’ growth prospects.

There has been pressure on the Health Care sector for the last couple of years, amid higher interest rates and shifting US policy under the Trump administration that has weighed on funding and sentiment. Some of those concerns lifted after the end of the quarter, with the announcement of an agreement with Pfizer (PFE) over most-favored-nation (MFN) drug pricing and the sale of drugs through a TrumpRx website that will have a limited impact on Pfizer’s profitability. However, the shutdown of the federal government in October centered on a dispute over the extension of some Affordable Care Act insurance-plan subsidies. All of this has left valuations at unusually attractive levels.

Health Care is the portfolio’s fourth-largest sector holding and one of its largest overweights. Our 11 Health Care companies span pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, life sciences, and equipment and services. They include Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), a US company that has a near-monopoly position in cystic-fibrosis treatments and a research-and-development engine that offers optionality on future drugs; Vertex’s earnings should compound about 15% annually over the next five years. We also added a new US holding this quarter, Elevance Health (ELV), a major medical-care organization. Readers may remember that we exited industry leader UnitedHealthcare (UNH) in May amid a criminal investigation into its Medicare Advantage business. Like UnitedHealthcare, Elevance enjoys a favorable industry structure—with an entrenched position in consolidated, regulated markets—but at a much more palatable valuation and with less legal risk.

In the US, growth in national health expenditures is projected to outpace that of GDP over the next decade, lifting the sector’s share of the economy above 20% by 2033.

While the market has mistaken the Health Care sector’s sales declines from pandemic-inflated levels as evidence of structural decline, there are now signs that a recovery is underway—in companies’ sales and in their share prices. Operating fundamentals in key end markets are stabilizing from pandemic dislocations.

Hospital finances improved in early 2025 from the year before as volumes normalized and revenue growth outpaced expenses, although the recovery in the US could still be rocky due to policy uncertainty. Medical technology and services companies whose products are tied to medical procedures are likely to see better pricing power and a more favorable sales mix if patient utilization of the health-care system continues to stabilize. Life sciences tools should also benefit from healing capital budgets. Finally, the capital cycle is turning more supportive. Dealmaking has returned to the sector amid increasing clarity on new US drug pricing implementation timelines. Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), the world’s largest provider of life-sciences tools, offers an early sign of a shift: although its results for the second quarter (reported in July) only modestly exceeded expectations, management forecast 3–6% organic revenue growth next year, and 7% beyond 2027—an outlook that was better than investors feared. The stock surged in response, and it was a solid performer for the quarter.

On several measures, the sector’s forward price-to-earnings ratio is at one of its widest discounts to the broader market in more than a decade, indicating profound pessimism about future profitable growth. That doesn’t square with the outlook for the portfolio’s Health Care holdings, whose revenues appear to be on the cusp of a cyclical recovery.

Performance and Attribution

The Global Equity composite rose 2.6% gross of fees in the third quarter, lagging the 7.7% gain in the MSCI ACWI Index. Year to date, the composite rose 10.95% gross of fees, compared with an index gain of 18.86%.

Portfolio Attribution by Sector

Stock selection was weak in several sectors, especially IT and Financials, while allocation provided only a modest benefit. Communication Services contained some bright spots.

Top contributors to relative performance:

In Communication Services, Alphabet’s stock gained after a favorable ruling in the Justice Department’s antitrust case, which spared the company from the worst outcome, a forced sale of its Android operating system or Chrome browser. Instead, remedies such as ending search-exclusivity agreements (e.g., its arrangement with Apple (AAPL)) and providing third parties access to its search index appear manageable.

Tencent, another Communication Services holding, reported strong quarterly results, including a 15% jump in revenue, year over year, as well as improved margins. Advertising remained a key source of growth, with AI-powered tools improving click-through rates and enabling the company to charge higher prices. Gaming revenue was also robust, reflecting healthy demand for new titles and continued interest in existing franchises.

In IT, our overweight in semiconductor and semiconductor-equipment holdings ASML (ASML) and Broadcom (AVGO) were helpful. The latter raised its outlook for next year, predicting that AI revenue growth will accelerate from this year’s rate of 50–60%. Management didn’t disclose its underlying revenue estimates but indicated a “fairly material” increase.

Top detractors from relative performance:

IT was a tale of two industries: while semiconductor and semiconductor-equipment stocks continued to perform well due to AI-related demand, software and services lagged amid fear of potential disruption by AI. Accenture, an IT-services holding, delivered good results; however, its outlook was disappointing due to slower growth in the IT budgets of large companies, which are proceeding more cautiously with some projects due to macroeconomic concerns while also redirecting capital toward AI-focused work. The reduced guidance added to concerns that AI disruption may become a net negative for Accenture. Shares of Globant (GLOB) have also struggled. Growth has slowed significantly over the past year, and while recent results met consensus expectations, they were accompanied by another downward revision to management’s forecasts. Even as the company’s pipeline remains strong, with aggregate estimated contract values up 25% year over year, customers have postponed implementations due to macroeconomic uncertainty. In Financials, electronic-trading platform Tradeweb (TW) said revenue climbed 25% but that electronic trading lost some market share to traditional “voice” trading, in which traders negotiate by the phone. This often occurs during periods of heightened volatility and is unlikely to signal that the digitalization of fixed-income markets in the US has reached its limit.

The US was responsible for 85% of the portfolio’s relative shortfall. The weakness in US holdings was only slightly ottset by strong picks in Emerging Markets (EMs).

Top contributors to relative performance:

In EMs, aside from Tencent, Taiwan-based Delta Electronics (OTCPK:DLEGF) was a positive contributor. The manufacturer of

power-management components beat earnings expectations by 15–20%, with particular strength in its power business and surging demand for liquid-cooling systems amid the construction of new AI data centers.

Taiwan’ s TSMC also reported strong results and projected full-year revenue growth of 30%, up from a previous forecast in the low-20% range, as the chipmaker benefits from robust demand for high-performance computing needed for AI. The competitive landscape in leading-edge chip technology remains favorable, with ongoing challenges at Intel and Samsung further strengthening TSMC’s pricing power with customers. While Japan detracted overall, Sony was a strong contributor. Shares of the electronics and media company surged ahead of the spin-ott of its financials businesses. Sony also posted strong quarterly results in its core businesses, including healthy demand for PlayStation hardware and content and continued traction for image sensors used in smartphones and automobiles.

Top detractors from relative performance:

Along with Tradeweb, Netflix and The Trade Desk dragged down returns in the US. Netflix’s subscriber growth and other signs of strength in its latest earnings report weren’t enough to satisfy the market’s high expectations, and so the stock pared back some of the strong gains achieved earlier in the year. Netflix’s revenue also has been surprisingly resilient in the face of slower consumer spending, likely due to the low cost, high usage, and perceived value of its product, which can lend its stock a safe-haven-like quality. Therefore, easing macroeconomic concerns, including around taritts, may have played a part in directing investors away from Netflix. Shares of The Trade Desk tumbled as softer-than-expected guidance added to fears over increased competition for digital-advertising budgets from Amazon Prime (which announced a partnership with Netflix) and new AI-based ad models.

data.title: Super Micro Computer Stock: With Margins In Free Fall, Here's What Comes Next
data.description:
Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) has had a bumpy year as the stock has struggled to recover from governance concerns following delayed regulatory filings and a probe from the Department of Justice. Shares have swung wildly, only to end up in around the same place as they were 12 months prior. This leaves investors to wonder what 2026 has in store. I continue to see SMCI as a dead weight stock in a burgeoning AI market.

In my last piece on SMCI nearly 8 months ago, I recommended against the stock as declining revenue, contracting margins, and ongoing concerns about company management weighed on investor sentiment. Since that was published, shares have gained about 15%, which is well below its peers, the NASDAQ (QQQ), and AI stocks as a whole. That article can be read here.

Let's start with the preliminary Q1 2026 financial results that Super Micro released Thursday morning. While the details were sparse, the company provided some revenue projections and other tidbits that the market apparently didn't like considering SMCI is down 8% as I write this.

Here's what amounts to basically the whole release:

  • Recent design wins in excess of $12B, requesting delivery in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026 (Q2 ’26). Company will provide further updates on our upcoming earnings call on expected Q2 '26 deliveries and revenues.
  • Design win upgrades pushed some expected Q1'26 revenue to Q2 '26, resulting in estimated revenue in Q1 '26 of $5B versus $6B-$7B guidance.
  • Seeing robust demand for Supermicro Nvidia GB300, B300, RTX Pro, AMD 355X LC, now starting to ship.

The headline here, in my opinion, is the quite massive miss on revenue -- expectations for $5 billion in Q1 is more than 20% lower at the midpoint than previous guidance and analyst consensus estimates. The explanation given for this miss is that some of this previously projected revenue isn't gone but was simply pushed to Q2 due to "design win upgrades." This is not the first time Super Micro has missed on revenue guidance over the last few quarters, which is pretty dire in this booming AI market.

However, what continues to be the sticking point in the bear thesis for me is the inability of the company to generate any sort of operating leverage. As revenue has scaled, profit margins have collapsed and continue to decline, indicating that either the market is too competitive or that Super Micro has not set its product offerings apart. Below is a chart showing revenue vs. profit margin over the last three years:

This should be setting off alarm bells in every investor's brain. Part of the reason this pattern is taking place, in my opinion, is that Super Micro's regulatory concerns have hurt its ability to truly ramp up its operations as potential customers might be wary to commit to large contracts and securing funding has been difficult due to the delayed filings and governance issues. In February of last year, the company agreed on terms to borrow cash via private placement of convertible bonds, which is not typically the borrowing you see from companies with a super stable balance sheet and underlines these funding issues.

And then again in August, just a couple months ago, Super Micro filed a form 10-K with the SEC stating their internal controls were inadequate. Here's the gist:

We have concluded that our internal control over financial reporting was not effective as of June 30, 2025 due to the existence of material weaknesses in such controls.

We also cannot assure you that additional material weaknesses in our internal control over financial reporting will not arise or be identified in the future.

Not only is the company suffering from material weakness in its income statement and its ability to scale operations, Super Micro, by its own admission, cannot guarantee the accuracy of its internal controls or financial statements. What exactly is the bull thesis here? That none of the governance issues will end up mattering, the company will be able to turn its operations around, and suddenly find margins in a market that's a race to the bottom on cost with, apparently, no economies of scale?

As far as I can see, the stock is a coin flip at best, not an investment. Management has so far demonstrated an inability to regain investor trust and continues to over-promise and under-deliver. In its preliminary Q1 report, Super Micro reiterated guidance of $33 billion for FY2026, which some quick math tells us would require $28 billion in the next three quarters alone. Will the company be able to suddenly go from $5 billion in Q1 to an average of more than $9 billion in every remaining quarter in FY2026? Consider me highly skeptical.

Investor Takeaway

Though it has been almost three quarters since my last article on SMCI, my thesis remains largely unchanged -- declining operating leverage, slowing revenue growth, and governance issues make the stock one to avoid. In a market where AI stocks are surging left and right on increasing revenue, strong margins, and long-term potential, I simply don't see the value in parking capital in this dead weight investment.

In the short-term, I expect SMCI to, at best, hover around the $50 range with potential for downside as the company, in my opinion, tracks behind its annual revenue guidance. In the medium term and over the long term, I try to avoid companies that struggle to scale their operations. Revenue growth is bottoming out and margins have fallen precipitously, which could mean a rather unpleasant reckoning in the quarters ahead if Super Micro has to erode margins further to maintain that growth. As such, I am reiterating a Sell on SMCI.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: LendingClub: As Deposits Grow, Profits Are Soaring (Upgrade)
data.description:
With the stock market looking frothy, I continue to encourage investors to look outside the Magnificent 7 (or for that matter, investing in the S&P 500 as a whole because it's so dominated by the top tech stocks), and to diversify by allocating more of our portfolios toward better-value small- and mid-cap stocks.

LendingClub (NYSE:LC), in my view, is a stock that has looked increasingly attractive. The consumer lending company has pivoted successfully into becoming more of a full-service bank, and its access to deposits has allowed the company to boost its net interest margins. Shares of LendingClub have shot up ~10% after reporting Q3 results, bringing immediate gains to a stock that has seen relatively sleepy trading patterns over the past year.

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on LendingClub in 2021, when the stock was trading in the low teens. The stock has underperformed the S&P 500's rise in that timeframe. However, I now view LendingClub as a much improved company, and one that still possesses the originations growth of a much younger startup. I'm raising my rating on the stock to a "Buy."

To me, these are all the core reasons to be long on LendingClub:

  • Strong originations growth. The company has been achieving 30%+ originations growth. We note as well that it has been successful at selling the large majority of these originations off, and holding barely over 20% of its new origination on its balance sheet, freeing up capital for further originations and de-risking its operations.
  • Deposit growth is lowering LendingClub's funding costs and boosting its net interest margins. The company offers attractive high-yield savings (at 4.20% APY, as of the time of writing) and checking accounts, completing its vision of becoming more of a full-service bank. The availability of lower-cost deposits (versus more expensive capital sources earlier on in LendingClub's history) has boosted its net interest margins and allowed for tremendous profit expansion.
  • High servicing margins. LendingClub also now services loans on behalf of other institutions, which makes up over half of its servicing portfolio and gives the company access to a high-margin, zero-risk recurring revenue stream.
  • Focus on efficiency. Over the past several years, LendingClub has undergone several rounds of layoffs that have made its corporate structure much leaner, allowing for incredible profit expansion.

Stay long here: In my view, LendingClub's post-Q3 rally is just the beginning of a longer upward correction for this stock.

Q3 download

Let's now go through LendingClub's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

LendingClub's revenue grew 32% y/y to $266.2 million, which beat Wall Street's expectations of $256.0 billion (+27% y/y) by a five-point margin. We note that non-interest income, driven principally by the company's growing servicing portfolio, increased 75% y/y.

Originations growth continues to be a large part of the appeal of this stock. LendingClub's consumer loan originations grew 37% y/y to $2.6 billion. As shown below, the company is only planning on holding 23% of these originations for investment, with the rest either already sold or earmarked for sale: down 4 points y/y, and reducing the company's balance sheet risk. And also as shown, the company is expecting a similar originations growth rate between 35%-41% y/y for Q4.

The company notes that a technology advantage is what is driving strong originations growth, as LendingClub can offer loans with more attractive terms than its peers while maintaining high credit quality. Per CEO Scott Sanborn's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

With competitive loan rates enabled by our sophisticated credit models and a fast, frictionless process, we continue to be very successful at attracting our target customers. In fact, when our loan offers are made side-by-side in a leading loan comparison site, we closed 50% more customers on average than the competition.

We continue to be disciplined in our underwriting. Our asset yield remains strong and our borrower base continues to perform well. In fact, we're delivering our originations growth while also demonstrating roughly 40% outperformance on credit versus our competitor set.

Consistent strong credit performance on a high-yielding asset class has allowed us to confidently build our balance sheet, which now stands at over $11 billion, delivering a durable, resilient revenue stream that nonbanks can't replicate. In fact, this quarter, we generated our highest ever net interest income of $158 million, enabled by a growing balance sheet and expanding net interest margin.

Healthy net interest spreads are also a key part of this story. LendingClub's expansion of its checking and savings products has allowed the company to fund a greater portion of its held-for-investment loans with low-cost deposits, and the company's net interest margins in Q3 expanded 55bps y/y to 6.18%:

This gives us tremendous confidence in LendingClub's ability to maintain high net interest income even in the wake of interest rate declines. We should also pay attention to LendingClub's improving efficiency ratios, which are the result of the company's consistent attention to rightsizing its workforce. Total expenses as a percentage of net revenue fell 640bps y/y to 61.1%:

Meanwhile, GAAP net income essentially tripled y/y to $44.3 million, with EPS of $0.37 beating Wall Street's expectations of $0.31 with 19% upside.

Valuation and key takeaways

For next year FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting LendingClub to hit $1.14 billion in total revenue (+14% y/y) and generate $1.50 in EPS (+37% y/y). This puts LendingClub's current valuation at just 12.3x FY26 P/E.

For a company that is currently growing revenue and earnings at a >30% clip, this is quite a discount in an expensive stock market. Historically, investors have viewed LendingClub as risky, with choppy execution trends. I'd argue now, however, that with the company boosting its net interest margin spreads and holding a smaller percentage of its loan originations on its balance sheet heavily de-risks this company.

Stay long here and keep riding the recent upside higher.

data.title: Worldline: Some Signs Of Stabilization, But Turnaround Protracted
data.description:
Worldline SA (OTCPK:WWLNF) (Euronext Paris: WLN) is still dealing with challenges. While there isn't an expectation of much more offboarding of high-risk business from this point onwards, and the merchant business overall is expected to be in an alright spot despite what will be lingering challenges with terminals, the bigger issue is the outlook for the financial services segment. After lapping tough comps, there is expected to be continued pressure on the business. There may also be a need for some investments to industrialize KYC, which could be a factor for the EBITDA guidance.

But other than that, things look to be stabilizing a bit, though the recovery won't be particularly quick. The cash pooling system that they use has also been addressed. The outstanding questions are the money laundering probe in Belgium, and solicitations are apparently occurring in Sweden in some audits. It's still a volatile issue and a battleground. It's risky, but we're already in it, and we want to wait, given the protracted turnaround.

Latest Results

The big news that markets responded to, other than some signs of operational stabilization, is the narrowing profit forecasts.

It is quite important for investors to be sensitive to this because it says quite a lot about the state of their HBR businesses, which are more involved and carry much higher margins. Indeed, the offboarding process is apparently nearing its conclusion for HBR. Management emphasized that all their peers also have some markets like this and that it is normal for a company of this scale.

The merchant business is sequentially stabilizing. Germany is a bit of an issue, but some of the JV-based businesses are driving a decent situation in other European geographies. The terminal businesses are still not doing well due to the fact that their terminals, due to supply issues, are not hitting the market quite in time for the upgrade cycles. So, this is a bit of a protracted issue, and margins are quite good for this business. That means a negative mix effect, and it is driving some churn that will need effort to be reversed in the future.

For the FS segment, card-based payments processing was stable, and there was some strength in upselling contracts and volume increases on existing customers. However, account payment activities brought down the segment. There are apparently not that many projects in the pipeline at the moment, and it will take a bit to restart the transformational projects that are a shortfall for the moment in the segment results. Also, some related milestones are less than guaranteed, also pressuring this part of the segment.

Bottom Line

There are still going to be some revenue pressures, which is why the revenue guidance hasn't narrowed. EBITDA guidance has narrowed, but mix effects are still negative and in play, and MSV growth hasn't been that great given macro softness in Europe.

On the cash pooling issue, there were concerns around the parent company making use of overdrafts as a liquidity source where credit facilities and other liquidity sources are more dependable. But the company explained the notional pooling concept, where subsidiaries don't need as much cash as they produce for working capital requirements and therefore can offer it at the parent level to deal with parent-level debts.

We are not sure how exotic such a structure is, but it's good that it's being scrutinized. The company has been using this system for 10 years, apparently. There is no co-mingling of merchant and corporate cash, according to management in the earnings call.

Disposals also continue. We mentioned the MeTS sale being at a slightly higher multiple than the broader business. They also sold their U.S. businesses, which include Bambora, a payment gateway that Shift4 is buying. It's at close to a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple, but only about 1% of forecast EBITDA (though 3% of the current EV). They are signaling more deals to come, likely to leave some geographies. It is a better environment right now for European dealmaking, so we don't see this as a badly timed capitulation.

In terms of valuation, the 3.15x EV/EBITDA is low relative to other payment peers like Nexi (OTCPK:NEXPF) at 5.7x. There is clearly a trust issue in Worldline, and they are underperforming peers due to this offboarding process and the issues in terminals. There are protracted issues that could take another year to resolve and drive some renewed positive performance. Other than maybe some more deals to come and the resolution of some of the outstanding probes, no major catalysts. The less commodified front-end segment should turn around in a year or two, and FS should also stabilize. The industry is still growing, and the compressed price may be a basis for some recovery, given not too much more bad news and a clearer outlook that will limit fuel for this quite crowded short.
